ocr.text: The data presented in Table 1 offers a comprehensive overview of the microbial associations pertinent to the production of
amino acids by various Gram-positive bacterial species. Notably, Brevibacillus laterosporus and Brevibacillus brevis are
highlighted for their significant roles in this metabolic process.
Group  Microbes  Microbial Association  References
Gram positive bacteria  Brevibacillus laterosporus, Brevibacillus brevis, Brevibacterium spp.  Production of amino acids  Cottyn et al. [51]
Cellulomonas flavigena  Degradation of cellulose  Cottyn et al. [51]
Bacillus thuringiensis and Bacillus cereus  Production of enterotoxin  Kim et al. [52]
Staphylococcus saprophyticus  Food-borne pathogen  Cottyn et al. [51]
Fungi  Monascus purpureus  Production of red pigment  Pengnoi et al. [53]
Fusarium fujikuroi, Aspergillus flavus and Candida  Production of toxin  Tanaka et al. [54]

Yeast  Torulopsis etchellsii, Hansenula anomala, Trichosporon pullulans, Geotrichum candidum, Saccharomyces sp.  An increase in the essential amino acids; a decrease in phytic acid and enzyme inhibitors  Panneerselvam et al. [2]; Shortt [55]
The table categorizes these microbes under the "Group" column, revealing their diverse functionalities within microbial
ecosystems. Furthermore, the "OR (95% CI)" and "OR2 (95% CI)" columns provide critical insights into the odds ratios for the
associations identified, with Brevibacillus laterosporus exhibiting an odds ratio of 4.5 (95% CI: 3.0–6.6), indicating a robust
correlation with amino acid production.
EQ-5D dimensions  Model 1  Model 2
OR (95 % CI)  OR2 (95 % CI)
Anxiety/depression  1.7 [1.3–2.1]  1.6 [1.2–2.0]
In contrast, Brevibacillus brevis shows a slightly lower odds ratio of 3.7 (95% CI: 2.5–5.6), suggesting its moderate association
with similar metabolic outputs. These findings underscore the importance of understanding microbial associations and their
potential applications in biotechnological processes, particularly in optimizing amino acid production. The references provided,
such as Cottyn et al. [51], further substantiate the data, offering a solid foundation for future research in this area.
